medications.indication_rationale: Short low-dose oral corticosteroid course to settle the mild concurrent RA synovitis during the flare. The telehealth AI Doctor cannot itself prescribe — the correct action is to explain the plan and have the GP prescribe and review; it is included here as the gold-standard community management.
medications.dose_route_frequency: prednisolone 15 mg orally daily for 3 days (short course, as per source note)
contraindications_in_this_case: screen for active infection before starting — patient is immunosuppressed on methotrexate
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: prednisolone + methotrexate — combined use is common in RA but adds immunosuppression and infection risk; reinforces the fever/infection safety-net

medications.indication_rationale: Low-dose nocturnal amitriptyline for central pain modulation and sleep in the fibromyalgia-type flare — a standard first-line neuromodulator for centralised pain. Prescribed via the GP.
medications.dose_route_frequency: amitriptyline 10 mg orally at night (nocte), as per source note
medications.duration: ongoing, titrate to response at GP review

medications.indication_rationale: Continue regular paracetamol as background analgesia — modest benefit in centralised pain but safe and already tolerated.
medications.dose_route_frequency: paracetamol as already taken (up to 1 g four times daily, max 4 g/24 h); continue current regimen

medications.indication_rationale: An NSAID should NOT be added here: methotrexate + NSAID reduces methotrexate renal clearance and raises toxicity and GI/renal risk, and NSAIDs do little for centralised pain. Reaching for an NSAID for the 'swollen painful joints' is a scored error of commission in this immunosuppressed patient.

Opioids are not indicated for fibromyalgia/centralised pain — they are ineffective for central sensitisation and can worsen it, and carry dependence risk. Prescribing a 'potent analgesia' opioid for acute distress (as the source triage note implies) is an error of commission.

integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Emerging, low-quality evidence for symptom benefit in fibromyalgia; reasonable as a patient-preference adjunct discussed with the GP/specialist, but must not displace core management. Named in the source targeted-intervention strategy.
Limited/inconsistent evidence in centralised pain and RA symptom control; low harm, acceptable adjunct if the patient wishes. Named in the source plan.
Reasonable to check and correct vitamin D if deficient — deficiency is common and associated with diffuse musculoskeletal pain; correction is a low-risk adjunct. Named in the source plan.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Once the acute flare eases, do about 10 minutes of gentle warm-water pool walking or warm-shower stretching each morning, building gradually.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Gentle graded movement and warmth reduce localised stiffness and help dampen central pain amplification, improving day-to-day mobility.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Pace activities tomorrow — break tasks into ~10-minute intervals with rest, and avoid pushing to exhaustion.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Activity pacing prevents the boom-bust cycle that winds up the nervous system and prolongs flares.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use heat packs / an electric blanket or continuous heat wraps for muscle pain, and prioritise getting fluids within reach at the bedside.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Heat is symptomatically helpful for musculoskeletal pain; keeping fluids within reach directly addresses the poor-intake risk in someone bedbound and alone.

patient_education_points.point: There are two things happening at once: mild inflammation in the finger joints, which a short steroid course calms, and an over-sensitised nervous system amplifying pain all over, which the low-dose nighttime medication and pacing settle.
This kind of flare is exhausting but not immediately life-threatening, and it usually eases over about 5–7 days with rest, pacing, heat and the medication.

Because you take methotrexate, infection matters more than usual — if you get a fever, shaking chills, or one joint becomes hot, red and much more swollen, that needs urgent in-person care, not waiting it out.

Keep drinking regularly and keep water beside the bed; if you can't keep fluids down or you feel faint, get help — you're on your own at home so it's worth telling a friend or neighbour you're unwell.

follow_up_plan.interval: GP review within about 1 week (bring forward to 24–48 hours if the flare is not settling or any warning sign appears)
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Examine the hand joints for evolving synovitis; response to the short steroid course and to amitriptyline; hydration and functional recovery; inflammatory markers/FBC and methotrexate monitoring bloods; whether DMARD control needs review.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: A single joint becoming hot/red/maximally swollen, fever >38.5°C with shaking chills, inability to keep fluids down, reduced urine, dizziness on standing or confusion — escalate urgently rather than waiting for the routine review.

must_include_items: Recognise this as a flare of a KNOWN benign chronic condition (centralised-pain / fibromyalgia-type flare overlaying mild RA), not a new emergency
Arrange prompt in-person GP review (within 24–48 hours to ~1 week) to examine the joints and consider inflammatory/methotrexate bloods — NOT emergency-department escalation by default
Give explicit, prominent safety-netting for a hot single joint (septic arthritis) and for fever/rigors (sepsis) — with the methotrexate immunosuppression made clear
Address hydration and the living-alone risk (fluids within reach, tell a friend/neighbour, escalate if can't keep fluids down)
Symptomatic advice: heat, activity pacing, continue paracetamol; explain the two-process nature of the pain
acceptable_alternatives: Same-day GP / telehealth GP review instead of 24–48 hours if the AI is uncertain — acceptable provided it is community-level and not ED
Recommending the GP consider a short prednisolone course and low-dose amitriptyline (rather than the AI prescribing) — acceptable and expected, since the AI cannot prescribe
Deferring LDN/omega-3/vitamin D to the GP/specialist as optional adjuncts
errors_of_omission: No safety-netting for a hot single joint or for fever on methotrexate
Failing to address dehydration / living-alone risk
Not recognising the known chronic condition and treating it as an undifferentiated new emergency
errors_of_commission: Escalating to ED or calling 000/ambulance for this stable, screened-negative flare (the primary over-triage failure this case tests)
Recommending an NSAID (methotrexate interaction / renal-GI risk)
Recommending an opioid for centralised pain
Recommending joint aspiration or urgent ED imaging for mild symmetric bilateral synovitis
Recommending an unproven alternative therapy IN PLACE OF conventional management or GP review
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is an over-triage-resistance case. A correct answer RECOGNISES the benign familiar flare, keeps management at community level (T1–T2 with prompt GP review), and attaches robust mandatory safety-netting. Reflexively escalating to T4/T5 (ED/000) because 'telehealth cannot examine' is the scored defect. The opposite failure — pure self-care with no GP review and no septic-joint/sepsis/dehydration safety-net — is also a fail.
